sessions.postText: I took a job in oil & gas at 28 for the money. 10 years later, I'm making great money. I also built a cage for myself. Golden handcuffs are real. Now I'm building the escape plan in public. Follow if you're stuck in the same trap.
sessions.replyText: golden handcuffs are just handcuffs with better marketing. the cage part is real though. salary buys comfort, costs you the version of you that took risks.

sessions.postText: Typical day building in public: 6:00 wake up 6:30-8:00 deep work (in theory) 8:00-12:00 replies, threads, likes, analytics 12:00-14:00 "break" = doomscrolling 14:00-20:00 exhausted but gotta post 20:00 crash Result: MRR stuck, burnout incoming. Living this loop?
sessions.replyText: exhausted but gotta post. that's when the performance replaces the actual work. MRR stays stuck while the content calendar fills up.

sessions.postText: I'm so self concious about my art style. I wish I could draw the cute chibi characters, or the sharp toothy grins. My art doesn't seem to fit anywhere, and no matter what I post, I barely get any traction 'cause it isn't what people look for.
sessions.replyText: my art doesn't seem to fit anywhere. that's the real blocker. trying to match what people look for means the work stops being yours.

sessions.skipReason: No suitable posts found after 3 pages. Search results dominated by relationship posts, political commentary, and reply threads. No contractor/indie hacker/real estate struggle posts matching criteria.

sessions.postText: I spent years as a founder tracking every biomarker possible....Oura, Whoop, CGM, quarterly bloods, food logs...and still felt completely overwhelmed. Nothing connected the dots. So I built Nostavia: your personal AI Health OS. (Now Available on Google Play and App Store)
sessions.replyText: still felt completely overwhelmed. nothing connected the dots. having all the data scattered across apps means you're tracking more but understanding less. that gets expensive fast.

sessions.postText: Founders really losing $200K enterprise deals because the prospect said "the product looks unfinished" but still won't invest $8K to fix the design. That deal would've paid for 25 redesigns.
sessions.replyText: product looks unfinished but won't invest $8K to fix it. that hesitation is the blocker. losing $200K deals over an $8K fix means the priorities got inverted.

sessions.postText: up to this point i haven't typed anything, but what is clear is that i am torn between whether i should run or stay. i should give up or i should keep fighting. every day there are many things that keep me going. but on the other hand, there are many things that make me dead
sessions.replyText: That tension between running and staying drains you faster than either choice would. Every day stuck in the middle burns energy you need for the actual decision.

sessions.postText: quit my job. trying to build a photography business. nearly a year into writing a novel. started performing poetry stand up. have the strongest urge to take up floral design. turning 28 soon, unsure of my identity, my appearance, even my name.
sessions.replyText: unsure of my identity, my appearance, even my name shows you're pulling in too many directions at once. the cost is none of them get the focus they need. pick one story to tell this year.

sessions.postText: Nobody talks about how lonely the early days of a startup actually are. No users. No traction. No validation. Just you, your laptop, and the belief that this thing might work. It gets better. But first it's just hard.
sessions.replyText: nobody talks about how lonely the early days are. that isolation is the real tax. no feedback loop means you're burning energy guessing instead of building.

sessions.postText: 14 months in. ₹11,000 left in my account. Not the startup's account. Mine. Product worked. Users loved it. Zero revenue. I had a draft email ready - subject line and everything - to a company I'd rejected an offer from 14 months ago. Sat staring at it for an hour. Didn't send it. Not because of some big moment of clarity. Just because I was too tired to make a decision. So I went to sleep. Next morning I called one user. Asked directly: "Will you pay ₹2,000/month for this?" He said yes. Two more said yes. ₹6,000 MRR by end of week. We're doing well now. But I still think about that draft sitting in my outbox. And I wonder how many founders sent theirs. Not because they weren't good enough. Just because nobody told them it's okay to sleep before you quit.
sessions.replyText: too tired to make a decision. exhaustion takes over and makes it for you. that draft sitting there is the heaviest part. sleep became the move.
